faculty.title: Professor
faculty.research_interests: Telecommunication, network systems design, wireless networks, wireless technologies.
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Anthony Acampora’s research centers on the design, analysis, and optimization of telecommunication and network systems, with a particular emphasis on wireless networks and emerging wireless technologies. His work combines theoretical modeling, simulation, and experimental testbeds to advance the performance, reliability, and scalability of modern communication infrastructures.

faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.research_interests: Cancer immunology; immune cell therapy; biomaterials; mechanotransduction
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Kolade Adebowale investigates how biophysical cues in the extracellular matrix shape immune cell function and cancer progression, integrating mechanotransduction, biomaterials engineering, and immune cell therapy. His work spans the mechanobiology of monocytes, macrophages, and T cells in viscoelastic environments, and translates these insights into engineered immune cell platforms for cancer immunotherapy.
populations: human immune cells (monocytes, macrophages, T cells)

recent_publications.title: Monocytes use protrusive forces to generate migration paths in viscoelastic collagen-based extracellular matrices.
recent_publications.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
recent_publications.abstract: Circulating monocytes are recruited to the tumor microenvironment, where they can differentiate into macrophages that mediate tumor progression. To reach the tumor microenvironment, monocytes must first extravasate and migrate through the type-1 collagen rich stromal matrix. The viscoelastic stromal matrix around tumors not only stiffens relative to normal stromal matrix, but often exhibits enhanced viscous characteristics, as indicated by a higher loss tangent or faster stress relaxation rate.

faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.research_interests: Biomaterials, tissue engineering, precision medicine, sex differences, cardiovascular disease, immunoengineering
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Brian Aguado’s research integrates biomaterials and tissue‑engineering approaches with precision‑medicine concepts to uncover how biological sex, age, and genetic sex‑chromosome factors drive cardiovascular disease. He develops engineered hydrogel platforms and nanostructured extracellular‑matrix cues to model sex‑specific myocardial fibrosis, aortic valve stenosis, and valvular calcification, and uses network‑based drug‑response modeling to identify personalized therapeutic strategies.
methodologies: in vitro cell culture of myofibroblasts and cardiac fibroblasts
high‑throughput drug screening on digital medicine platforms
precision biomaterials design incorporating demographic descriptors

funded_grants.title: CAREER: Exploring X-chromosome inactivation dynamics in myofibroblasts using engineered hydrogels
funded_grants.abstract: Sex differences in health and disease have often been attributed to sex hormones, but there is a growing appreciation that sex chromosomes directly impact sex differences in disease progression. The goal of this CAREER project is to improve the understanding of how two X chromosomes in female cells contribute to female-specific aortic valve stenosis progression, the most common aortic valve disorder that can lead to sudden heart failure. recent_publications.title: Precision Biomaterials: Incorporating Sex, Age, and Social Descriptors in Biomaterials Research.
Determining sex differences in aortic valve myofibroblast responses to drug combinations identified using a digital medicine platform.
recent_publications.abstract: Aortic valve stenosis (AVS) is a sexually dimorphic disease where aortic valve leaflets develop fibrosis and calcification, leading to heart failure if untreated. Sex differences in AVS progression depend on valvular interstitial cells (VICs) activating to myofibroblasts that drive aberrant extracellular matrix remodeling. To date, no treatment strategies have leveraged cellular sex differences to determine drug combinations that target VIC myofibroblast activation. Here, we harnessed IDentif.AI

recent_publications.title: Y chromosome-linked UTY modulates sex differences in valvular fibroblast methylation in response to nanoscale extracellular matrix cues.
recent_publications.abstract: Aortic valve stenosis (AVS) is a progressive disease, wherein males more often develop valve calcification relative to females that develop valve fibrosis. Valvular interstitial cells (VICs) aberrantly activate to myofibroblasts during AVS, driving the fibrotic valve phenotype in females. Myofibroblasts further differentiate into osteoblast-like cells and produce calcium nanoparticles, driving valve calcification in males. We hypothesized that the lysine demethylase UTY (ubiquitously transcribed

recent_publications.title: Substrate stiffness modulates cardiac fibroblast activation, senescence, and proinflammatory secretory phenotype.
recent_publications.journal: American journal of physiology. Heart and circulatory physiology
recent_publications.abstract: In vitro cultures of primary cardiac fibroblasts (CFs), the major extracellular matrix (ECM)-producing cells of the heart, are used to determine molecular mechanisms of cardiac fibrosis. However, the supraphysiologic stiffness of tissue culture polystyrene (TCPS) triggers the conversion of CFs into an activated myofibroblast-like state, and serial passage of the cells results in the induction of replicative senescence. These phenotypic switches confound the interpretation of experimental data ob

recent_publications.title: Equitable hiring strategies towards a diversified faculty.
Osteopontin activity modulates sex-specific calcification in engineered valve tissue mimics.
recent_publications.abstract: Patients with aortic valve stenosis (AVS) have sexually dimorphic phenotypes in their valve tissue, where male valvular tissue adopts a calcified phenotype and female tissue becomes more fibrotic. The molecular mechanisms that regulate sex-specific calcification in valvular tissue remain poorly understood. Here, we explored the role of osteopontin (OPN), a pro-fibrotic but anti-calcific bone sialoprotein, in regulating the calcification of female aortic valve tissue. Recognizing that OPN mediate

recent_publications.title: Correction: Inflammatory serum factors from aortic valve stenosis patients modulate sex differences in valvular myofibroblast activation and osteoblast-like differentiation.
recent_publications.abstract: Correction for 'Inflammatory serum factors from aortic valve stenosis patients modulate sex differences in valvular myofibroblast activation and osteoblast-like differentiation' by Brandon J. Vogt

recent_publications.title: Inflammatory serum factors from aortic valve stenosis patients modulate sex differences in valvular myofibroblast activation and osteoblast-like differentiation.
recent_publications.abstract: Aortic valve stenosis (AVS) is a sexually dimorphic cardiovascular disease that is driven by fibrosis and calcification of the aortic valve leaflets. Circulating inflammatory factors present in serum from AVS patients contribute to sex differences in valve fibro-calcification by driving the activation of valvular interstitial cells (VICs) to myofibroblasts and/or osteoblast-like cells. However, the molecular mechanisms by which inflammatory factors contribute to sex-specific valve fibro-calcific

recent_publications.title: Network modeling predicts personalized gene expression and drug responses in valve myofibroblasts cultured with patient sera.
recent_publications.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
recent_publications.abstract: Aortic valve stenosis (AVS) patients experience pathogenic valve leaflet stiffening due to excessive extracellular matrix (ECM) remodeling. Numerous microenvironmental cues influence pathogenic expression of ECM remodeling genes in tissue-resident valvular myofibroblasts, and the regulation of complex myofibroblast signaling networks depends on patient-specific extracellular factors. Here, we combined a manually curated myofibroblast signaling network with a data-driven transcription factor netw

faculty.title: Professor
faculty.research_interests: Computational Biology; Cancer Genomics; Mutagenesis; Ageing; Bioinformatics
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Ludmil Alexandrov’s research combines computational biology and bioinformatics to investigate the genomic underpinnings of cancer and ageing. He focuses on deciphering mutational processes and patterns in cancer genomes, exploring how mutagenesis contributes to tumor development and age‑related genomic alterations.

recent_publications.title: Multi-omics profiling reveals microenvironmental remodeling as a key driver of house dust mite-induced lung cancer progression.
recent_publications.abstract: Chronic exposure to the common aeroallergen house dust mite (HDM) induces lung inflammation and DNA damage, but its impact on lung cancer development remains largely unexplored. Using whole-genome sequencing, RNA-seq, and DNA methylation profiling, we assessed HDM effects in lung epithelial cell lines and a mouse orthotopic lung cancer model. recent_publications.title: Revealing the Drivers Underlying Distinct Evolutionary Trajectories in Lung Adenocarcinoma.
recent_publications.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
recent_publications.abstract: Elucidating the evolution of cancers allows us to understand their key events, and the order in which they occur. To chart and interpret these evolutionary trajectories, we leverage whole-genome sequencing of lung tumours, including those from the largest cohort to date of lung cancers in subjects who have never smoked. recent_publications.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
recent_publications.abstract: Identifying mutational signatures is a key component of cancer genomics studies, yet the influence of variant calling strategies on signature extraction has not been systematically evaluated. recent_publications.title: Chromothripsis and ecDNA initiated by N4BP2 nuclease fragmentation of cytoplasm-exposed chromosomes.
recent_publications.abstract: Genome instability, including chromothripsis, is a hallmark of cancer. Cancer cells frequently contain micronuclei-small, nucleus-like structures formed by chromosome missegregation-that are susceptible to rupture, exposing chromatin to cytoplasmic nucleases. recent_publications.abstract: Understanding tumor cell dynamics can improve prognosis and treatment but remains limited for lung adenocarcinoma in people who have never smoked (NS-LUAD). recent_publications.abstract: The identification and classification of carcinogens is critical in cancer epidemiology, necessitating updated methodologies to manage the burgeoning biomedical literature. We introduce the Carcinogen Detection via Transformers (CarD-T) framework, combining transformer-based machine learning with probabilistic analysis to efficiently nominate potential carcinogens from scientific texts. recent_publications.title: Panorama of Chromosomal Instability in Lung Cancer.
recent_publications.journal: medRxiv : the preprint server for health sciences
recent_publications.abstract: Lung cancer is a highly heterogeneous disease primarily driven by tobacco smoking. About 20% of lung cancers occur among patients who have never smoked (LCINS) with differences in patient ancestry, sex, tumor histology, and clinical features. Our understanding of chromosomal instability in lung cancer, especially LCINS, is still limited. Here, we perform a comprehensive study of 182,429 somatic structural variations (SVs) detected in 1,209 whole-genome sequenced lung cancers, of which 864 LCINS.

recent_publications.title: The DREAM complex links somatic mutation, lifespan, and disease.
recent_publications.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
recent_publications.abstract: The DREAM complex has emerged as a central repressor of DNA repair, raising questions as to whether such repression exerts long-term effects on human health. Here we establish that DREAM activity significantly impacts lifetime somatic mutation burden, and that such effects are linked to altered lifespan and age-related disease pathology. recent_publications.title: A prognostic signature for lung adenocarcinoma in people who have never smoked.
recent_publications.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
recent_publications.abstract: Knowledge of tumor cell dynamics can inform prognosis and treatment yet is largely lacking for lung adenocarcinoma in people who have never smoked (NS-LUAD). With RNA-seq data from 684 NS-LUAD and validation in an independent dataset, we identified three subtypes with distinct phenotypic traits and cell compositions. Additional genomic and histological data further characterized the subtypes.

recent_publications.title: Improved Mutation Detection in Duplex Sequencing Data with Sample-Specific Error Profiles.
recent_publications.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
recent_publications.abstract: Duplex sequencing enables highly accurate detection of rare somatic mutations, but existing variant callers often rely on protocol-specific heuristics that limit sensitivity, reproducibility, and cross-study comparability. We present DupCaller, a probabilistic variant caller that builds sample-specific error profiles and applies a strand-aware statistical model for mutation detection. recent_publications.title: Evaluating topography of mutational signatures with SigProfilerTopography.
recent_publications.abstract: The mutations found in a cancer genome are shaped by diverse processes, each displaying a characteristic mutational signature that may be influenced by the genome's architecture. While prior analyses have evaluated the effect of topographical genomic features on mutational signatures, there has been no computational tool that can comprehensively examine this interplay. faculty.research_interests_enriched: Ilkay Altintas De Callafon focuses on developing AI‑driven, data‑intensive tools and educational resources that accelerate scientific discovery across domains. Their work spans contextual‑bandit and federated learning methods for hardware prediction and digital‑twin creation, AI‑aided automation of research workflows, and the integration of physiological and ecological data (e.g., wearable activity monitoring, plant‑fire interactions) to support health and environmental decision‑making.

faculty.title: Professor
faculty.research_interests: Curriculum and program design for computer science education, with a focus on better serving underrepresented groups.
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Christine Alvarado focuses on designing and evaluating computer science curricula and programs that improve equity and retention for underrepresented undergraduate students. Her work examines gender pathways, integrates large language models into introductory CS courses, and develops scalable early‑research scholar models for large‑scale institutions. She also investigates faculty promotion challenges and broader educational outcomes using mixed quantitative and qualitative methods.

funded_grants.title: Collaborative Research: Equity of Access to Computer Science: Factors Impacting the Characteristics and Success of Undergraduate CS Majors
funded_grants.abstract: This project aims to serve the national interest by improving undergraduate computer science education.  It will do so by completing a research study that can reveal potential systemic limitations in access to computer science education by all students.  This research study will examine ten-years of undergraduate student application, admissions, and retention data from four institutions.  Analysis of these data will describe how students of varying demographics and pre-college preparation are pr

funded_grants.title: Collaborative Research: Scaling the Early Research Scholars Program
funded_grants.abstract: Enrollment in computer science (CS) programs across the nation is higher than ever, yet two critical problems persist.  First, despite these high enrollments, relatively few U.S. students are continuing on to graduate school.  Maintaining and improving the health of the domestic Ph.D. pipeline is a concern of universities, companies, and government agencies. Second, the population of students pursuing CS degrees is not representative of the diversity of people in the U.S., with women and other g

funded_grants.title: EAGER: The Experience of a Small College, at a Large Scale
funded_grants.abstract: This project, at the University of California-San Diego, will develop a model for computer science classes at large research universities that incorporates lessons from both MOOCs and high-touch classes at small institutions.  The project team will use a combination of lecture, tutors, teaching assistants (TAs), flipped classroom, and peer instruction within the large class setting. funded_grants.title: BP: CSE Early Research Scholars Program
funded_grants.abstract: The University of California San Diego (UCSD) will implement a structured early-college research program to increase retention in Computer Science and Engineering (CSE).  During their first and second years in CSE, students will participate in team-based research projects. recent_publications.abstract: Prior research examining the relationship between cannabis use, sedentary behavior, and physical activity has generated conflicting findings, potentially due to biases in the self-reported measures used to assess physical activity. This study aimed to more precisely explore the relationship between cannabis use and sedentary behavior/physical activity using objective measures. recent_publications.abstract: Adult spinal deformity (ASD) is an increasingly prevalent disorder in the aging population. Surgical intervention is a common and generally effective treatment for severe cases. However, it is associated with relatively high rates of complications, one of the most common, and devastating of which is proximal junctional failure (PJF). PJF is characterized by symptomatic mechanical failure at the junction of the spinal fusion construct and the adjacent proximal mobile spinal segments, leading to a

recent_publications.title: Evidence gaps among systematic reviews examining the relationship of race, ethnicity, and social determinants of health with adult inpatient quality measures.
recent_publications.abstract: The field of healthcare epidemiology is increasingly focused on identifying, characterizing, and addressing social determinants of health (SDOH) to address inequities in healthcare quality. To identify evidence gaps, we examined recent systematic reviews examining the association of race, ethnicity, and SDOH with inpatient quality measures. A retrospective cohort study was performed to provide further multicenter real-world evidence on dalbavancin use as a sequential therapy for Gram-positive BSI. recent_publications.title: Projecting Climate Dependent Coastal Flood Risk With a Hybrid Statistical Dynamical Model.
recent_publications.abstract: Numerical models for tides, storm surge, and wave runup have demonstrated ability to accurately define spatially varying flood surfaces. However these models are typically too computationally expensive to dynamically simulate the full parameter space of future oceanographic, atmospheric, and hydrologic conditions that will constructively compound in the nearshore to cause both extreme event and nuisance flooding during the 21st century. recent_publications.title: Human Health and Ocean Pollution.
recent_publications.abstract: Pollution - unwanted waste released to air, water, and land by human activity - is the largest environmental cause of disease in the world today. It is responsible for an estimated nine million premature deaths per year, enormous economic losses, erosion of human capital, and degradation of ecosystems. recent_publications.title: The BRAIN Initiative: developing technology to catalyse neuroscience discovery.
recent_publications.journal: Philosophical transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological sciences
recent_publications.abstract: The evolution of the field of neuroscience has been propelled by the advent of novel technological capabilities, and the pace at which these capabilities are being developed has accelerated dramatically in the past decade. Capitalizing on this momentum, the United States launched the Brain Research through Advancing Innovative Neurotechnologies (BRAIN) Initiative to develop and apply new tools and technologies for revolutionizing our understanding of the brain. In this article, we review the sci

recent_publications.title: Challenges and possible solutions to colorectal cancer screening for the underserved.
recent_publications.journal: Journal of the National Cancer Institute
recent_publications.abstract: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is a leading cause of cancer mortality worldwide. CRC incidence and mortality can be reduced through screening. However, in the United States, screening participation remains suboptimal, particularly among underserved populations such as the uninsured, recent immigrants, and racial/ethnic minority groups. Increasing screening rates among underserved populations will reduce the US burden of CRC. In this commentary focusing on underserved populations, we highlight the publi

recent_publications.title: Characterization of a novel glucokinase activator in rat and mouse models.
recent_publications.abstract: Glucokinase (GK) is a hexokinase isozyme that catalyzes the phosphorylation of glucose to glucose-6-phosphate. Glucokinase activators are being investigated as potential diabetes therapies because of their effects on hepatic glucose output and/or insulin secretion. Here, we have examined the efficacy and mechanisms of action of a novel glucokinase activator, GKA23. In vitro, GKA23 increased the affinity of rat and mouse glucokinase for glucose, and increased glucose uptake in primary rat hepatoc

recent_publications.title: An imprinted rheumatoid arthritis methylome signature reflects pathogenic phenotype.
recent_publications.abstract: A DNA methylation signature has been characterized that distinguishes rheumatoid arthritis (RA) fibroblast like synoviocytes (FLS) from osteoarthritis (OA) FLS. The presence of epigenetic changes in long-term cultured cells suggest that rheumatoid FLS imprinting might contribute to pathogenic behavior. To understand how differentially methylated genes (DMGs) might participate in the pathogenesis of RA, we evaluated the stability of the RA signature and whether DMGs are enriched in specific pathw

recent_publications.title: Nanoparticle-delivered multimeric soluble CD40L DNA combined with Toll-Like Receptor agonists as a treatment for melanoma.
recent_publications.abstract: Stimulation of CD40 or Toll-Like Receptors (TLR) has potential for tumor immunotherapy. Combinations of CD40 and TLR stimulation can be synergistic, resulting in even stronger dendritic cell (DC) and CD8+ T cell responses. To evaluate such combinations, established B16F10 melanoma tumors were injected every other day X 5 with plasmid DNA encoding a multimeric, soluble form of CD40L (pSP-D-CD40L) either alone or combined with an agonist for TLR1/2 (Pam(3)CSK(4) ), TLR2/6 (FSL-1 and MALP2), TLR3 (

recent_publications.title: Heart disease is common in humans and chimpanzees, but is caused by different pathological processes.
recent_publications.abstract: Heart disease is common in both humans and chimpanzees, manifesting typically as sudden cardiac arrest or progressive heart failure. Surprisingly, although chimpanzees are our closest evolutionary relatives, the major cause of heart disease is different in the two species. Histopathology data of affected chimpanzee hearts from two primate centers, and analysis of literature indicate that sudden death in chimpanzees (and in gorillas and orangutans) is commonly associated with diffuse interstitial

recent_publications.title: Caspase 8 promotes peripheral localization and activation of Rab5.
recent_publications.abstract: Caspase 8 is a cysteine protease that initiates apoptotic signaling via the extrinsic pathway in a manner dependent upon association with early endosomes. Previously, we identified caspase 8 as an effector of migration, promoting motility in a manner dependent upon phosphorylation on Tyr-380 by Src family kinases and its subsequent association with Src homology 2 domain-containing proteins. Here we demonstrate the regulation of the small GTPase Rab5, which mediates early endosome formation, homo

recent_publications.title: DNA poised for release in bacteriophage phi29.
recent_publications.abstract: We present here the first asymmetric, three-dimensional reconstruction of a tailed dsDNA virus, the mature bacteriophage phi29, at subnanometer resolution. This structure reveals the rich detail of the asymmetric interactions and conformational dynamics of the phi29 protein and DNA components, and provides novel insight into the mechanics of virus assembly. For example, the dodecameric head-tail connector protein undergoes significant rearrangement upon assembly into the virion. Specific interac

recent_publications.title: Human-specific regulation of alpha 2-6-linked sialic acids.
recent_publications.abstract: Many microbial pathogens and toxins recognize animal cells via cell surface sialic acids (Sias) that are alpha 2-3- or alpha 2-8-linked to the underlying glycan chain. Human influenza A/B viruses are unusual in preferring alpha 2-6-linked Sias, undergoing a switch from alpha 2-3 linkage preference during adaptation from animals to humans. This correlates with the expression of alpha 2-6-linked Sias on ciliated human airway epithelial target cells and of alpha 2-3-linked Sias on secreted soluble

faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.research_interests: Expertise in vehicle design, navigation, and control.
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Mark Anderson’s research centers on the design, navigation, and control of ground and aerial vehicles. He develops advanced control algorithms, sensor‑fusion techniques, and path‑planning methods to enhance vehicle performance, safety, and autonomy. His work integrates computational modeling, simulation, and experimental prototyping to address challenges in vehicular dynamics and intelligent transportation systems.

recent_publications.title: A cluster of acidic residues in the cytoplasmic domain of SARS-CoV-2 Spike is required for virion-incorporation and infectivity.
recent_publications.abstract: Like all coronaviruses, the infectivity of SARS-CoV-2 virus particles (virions) requires incorporation of the Spike glycoprotein. Yet, the mechanisms that support the virion-incorporation of Spike are incompletely defined. We noted an unusual feature of human sarbecovirus Spike proteins: their cytoplasmic domains (CDs) contain a stretch of acidic amino acids (DEDDSE). This sequence resembles a cluster of acidic residues, or acidic cluster (AC) motif, found in the cytoplasmic domain of the cellul

recent_publications.title: Antecedent moisture enhances early warning of atmospheric river flood hazards.
recent_publications.abstract: Atmospheric rivers are narrow bands of water vapor transport and serve as key drivers of water supplies and flood hazards in mid-latitude regions. The atmospheric river scale supports early-warning communication by ranking events from potentially beneficial to hazardous based on atmospheric forcing represented by water vapor transport magnitude and duration. However, the scale does not consider land-surface conditions that can influence how precipitation derived from water vapor translates into

recent_publications.abstract: Financial navigation could mitigate or prevent cancer-related financial hardship, but its effectiveness has not been established in real-world settings. We developed an intervention that provided proactive, personalized financial navigation to individuals newly diagnosed with cancer. We conducted a randomized controlled trial at two integrated health systems. Inclusion criteria were age 18+ years and receiving care for a new cancer diagnosis of any type or stage. recent_publications.title: Usability Testing an Educational Telemedicine Simulation: Description of Methods and Preliminary Findings.
recent_publications.journal: Studies in health technology and informatics
recent_publications.abstract: Simulations are often used in medical education to practice developing skills in low-stakes situations. We created a telemedicine simulation for residents in obstetrics, gynaecology, and family medicine to teach best practices in telehealth care. We wrote a case of a patient with gestational hypertension that tested resident performance in eight published telehealth competencies. Given the project's complexity, we conducted a usability test before piloting. Combining several user testing methods

recent_publications.title: Putting computational models of immunity to the test-An invited challenge to predict B.pertussis vaccination responses.
recent_publications.abstract: Systems vaccinology studies have been used to build computational models that predict individual vaccine responses and identify the factors contributing to differences in outcome. Comparing such models is challenging due to variability in study designs. To address this, we established a community resource to compare models predicting B. pertussis booster responses and generate experimental data for the explicit purpose of model evaluation. recent_publications.title: External Validation of a Machine Learning Model to Diagnose Kawasaki Disease.
recent_publications.abstract: We investigated the generalizability of a machine learning model trained to predict Kawasaki disease using laboratory and clinical data. The algorithm performed with >89% accuracy at 3 children's hospitals across the United States, demonstrating its potential as a physician support tool for diagnosing Kawasaki disease.

We evaluated the association between computed tomography (CT)-measurements of adipose tissue and plasma levels of PTX-3. We performed a cross-sectional analysis of community-dwelling adults enrolled in the multi-center Multiethnic Study of Atherosclerosis who underwent cardiac or abdominal CT and had available PTX-3 measurements. recent_publications.title: Implementing a Family-Centered Rounds Intervention Using Novel Mentor-Trios.
recent_publications.abstract: Patient and Family Centered I-PASS (PFC I-PASS) emphasizes family and nurse engagement, health literacy, and structured communication on family-centered rounds organized around the I-PASS framework (Illness severity-Patient summary-Action items-Situational awareness-Synthesis by receiver). We assessed adherence, safety, and experience after implementing PFC I-PASS using a novel "Mentor-Trio" implementation approach with multidisciplinary parent-nurse-physician teams coaching sites. recent_publications.title: Training simulated participants for role portrayal and feedback practices in communication skills training: A BEME scoping review: BEME Guide No. 86.
recent_publications.abstract: Providing feedback is a key aspect of simulated participants' (SPs) educational work. In teaching contexts, the ability to provide feedback to learners is central to their role. Suboptimal feedback practices may deny learners the valuable feedback they need to learn and improve. This scoping review systematically maps the evidence related to SPs' role as educators and identifies how SPs prepare for their role and feedback practices. The authors conducted a scoping review and included a group of

recent_publications.title: Impact of COVID-19 on electroconvulsive therapy practice across Canadian provinces during the first wave of the pandemic.
recent_publications.abstract: Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is a procedural treatment that is potentially life-saving for some patients with severe psychiatric illness. At the start of the global coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic, ECT practice was remarkably disrupted, putting vulnerable individuals at increased risk of symptom exacerbation and death by suicide. This study aimed to capture the self-reported experiences of psychiatrists based at healthcare facilities across Canadian provinces who were delivering

recent_publications.title: A CRISPR endonuclease gene drive reveals distinct mechanisms of inheritance bias.
recent_publications.abstract: CRISPR/Cas gene drives can bias transgene inheritance through different mechanisms. Homing drives are designed to replace a wild-type allele with a copy of a drive element on the homologous chromosome. recent_publications.title: Exploring a Tetrahydroquinoline Antimalarial Hit from the Medicines for Malaria Pathogen Box and Identification of its Mode of Resistance as PfeEF2.
recent_publications.abstract: New antimalarial treatments with novel mechanism of action are needed to tackle Plasmodium falciparum infections that are resistant to first-line therapeutics. Here we report the exploration of MMV692140 (2) from the Pathogen Box, a collection of 400 compounds that was made available by Medicines for Malaria Venture (MMV) in 2015. recent_publications.title: The Cancer Financial Experience (CAFÉ) study: randomized controlled trial of a financial navigation intervention to address cancer-related financial hardship.
recent_publications.abstract: There is an urgent need for evidence on how interventions can prevent or mitigate cancer-related financial hardship. Our objectives are to compare self-reported financial hardship, quality of life, and health services use between patients receiving a financial navigation intervention versus a comparison group at 12 months follow-up, and to assess patient-level factors associated with dose received of a financial navigation intervention. The Cancer Financial Experience (CAFÉ) study is a multi-sit

faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.research_interests: Co-design of optical systems and algorithms; computational optical systems
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Nicholas Antipa focuses on the integrated design of optical hardware and computational algorithms, developing computational optical systems that combine photonic components with advanced signal processing. His work spans co‑design of lenses, sensors, and imaging architectures, leveraging numerical modeling, algorithm optimization, and experimental prototyping to create next‑generation imaging and sensing technologies.

faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Kiana Aran develops nano‑electronic and transistor‑based platforms for real‑time, high‑throughput monitoring of CRISPR/Cas enzyme activity and nucleic‑acid detection, aiming to improve diagnostics and therapeutic monitoring for monogenic diseases such as sickle cell disease and infectious agents like SARS‑CoV‑2. Her work integrates biophysical single‑molecule techniques, real‑time electronic biosensing, and multi‑omics transistor technologies to elucidate enzyme kinetics and enable multiplexed assays. She also applies these tools to aging research, investigating erythrocyte contributions to blood‑brain barrier integrity, and contributes to semiconductor workforce development initiatives.
methodologies: in vitro CRISPR editing efficiency measurement

funded_grants.abstract: The United States semiconductor industry is facing a critical shortage of skilled workers, which threatens the nation’s ability to remain competitive in fields ranging from healthcare and defense to energy and communication. This project, the Semiconductor Workforce Advancement Nexus (SWAN), aims to address this urgent challenge by creating a scalable, university-based training model that focuses on technical training in collaboration with industry. funded_grants.abstract: The knowledge of how biological molecules interact with each other is essential to understanding their roles in sustaining life. Molecular interactions are commonly measured using optical techniques and specialized optical labels on the molecules of interest. Recent advances in modern electronics and nanoscale materials potentially allow continuous monitoring of molecular interactions in a more natural state without optical labels and instruments. recent_publications.abstract: CRISPR-Cas9 enables curative genome editing but requires precise control of target recognition, particularly when single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) influence specificity. Conventional biochemical and optical assays often rely on endpoint or ensemble-averaged measurements and therefore fail to resolve the real-time binding dynamics underlying off-target interactions. Here, we report a label-free, non-faradaic electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (nfEIS) platform that directly monitors

faculty.title: Professor
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Robert Asaro investigates the fundamental mechanisms governing mechanical behavior across scales, from the nanoscale stability and deformation of ultrafine-grained metals to the mechanosensitivity of cellular adhesion complexes. His work integrates experimental mechanics, advanced microscopy, and biophysical assays to elucidate how microstructural features and force transmission pathways control material performance and cell traction stresses. This interdisciplinary focus supports the development of stronger, more reliable nanostructured materials and advances understanding of force‑dependent cellular processes.

This project is concerned with fundamental studies of the stability of ultra-fine and nanstructured grains in metals, both with face centered cubic and hexagonal close packed crystal structures, and in metals that contain either, or both, nano-sized grains or nano-sized twins. Our model adhesome consists of the actin cytoskeleton-vinculin-talin-integrin-ligand-extracellular matrix-substrate force train. We specifically consider the effects of substrate stiffness on the force levels expected along the train and on the traction stresses they create at the substrate. faculty.title: Professor
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Peter Asbeck’s research centers on the development of next‑generation semiconductor devices and high‑speed integrated circuits, with a particular emphasis on III‑V tunneling MOSFETs, heterostructure material growth, and advanced device characterization using custom high‑speed instrumentation. He also applies his expertise in electromagnetic device design to biomedical engineering, exemplified by the design and validation of miniaturized repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) head coils for human neuromodulation studies.

recent_publications.abstract: Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) is a rapidly developing therapeutic modality for the safe and effective treatment of neuropsychiatric disorders. However, clinical rTMS driving systems and head coils are large, heavy, and expensive, so miniaturized, affordable rTMS devices may facilitate treatment access for patients at home, in underserved areas, in field and mobile hospitals, on ships and submarines, and in space. The central component of a portable rTMS system is a miniatur

faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Nikolay Atanasov focuses on developing safe autonomous robotic systems that can operate in unknown and dynamically changing environments. His work integrates Bayesian perception, representation learning, and control-theoretic safety guarantees to enable reliable navigation, mapping, and multi‑object tracking for aerial, ground, and legged robots. He also applies distributed learning and robust control to mission‑critical applications such as early wildfire detection and autonomous operation in construction, mining, and agricultural settings.
methodologies: field deployment and sensor network monitoring

funded_grants.abstract: Artificial perception techniques, allowing robot systems to know their location and surroundings using sensory data, have been instrumental for enabling robot automation outside of carefully controlled manufacturing settings. funded_grants.abstract: Autonomous robot systems offer tremendous potential for transforming various industry sectors, including transportation, construction, mining, and agriculture. faculty.title: Professor
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Scott Baden’s research centers on the development of scalable high‑performance computing frameworks and software infrastructures that enable cyber‑enabled scientific discovery across domains such as geophysical fluid dynamics, computational biology, and materials science. He creates novel parallel programming models (e.g., lattice parallelism), domain‑specific languages, and computational databases to accelerate large‑scale simulations of ocean mixing, cellular physiology, and first‑principles material design on hierarchical multiprocessor and exascale systems.

However, their complexity leads to an array of tradeoffs in writing programs that hinders timely implementation. These materials typically have complex structures and compositions, a large number of atoms (n>100) and little or no symmetry. recent_publications.abstract: Achieving a simple yet sustainable printing technique with minimal instruments and energy remains challenging.
